item.title: Teachers on frontline of HIV response – NSAAC
item.description: FIJI’S escalating HIV epidemic is increasingly affecting young people, with teachers now on the frontline of identifying and supporting at-risk students, the National Substance Abuse Advisory Council (NSAAC) has warned. Speaking at the School Management Association of Fiji conference in Suva this week, acting chief executive Josua Naisele said schools had become a critical point for early interven
item.content:encoded:
FIJI’S escalating HIV epidemic is increasingly affecting young people, with teachers now on the frontline of identifying and supporting at-risk students, the National Substance Abuse Advisory Council (NSAAC) has warned.

Speaking at the School Management Association of Fiji conference in Suva this week, acting chief executive Josua Naisele said schools had become a critical point for early intervention as HIV infections continued to surge among young Fijians.

“This is happening in our schools now. Teachers are on the front line of early detection and referral,” he said.

Mr Naisele said Fiji had recorded 5676 cumulative HIV cases by the end of 2025, including 2016 new infections last year alone.

He said HIV cases among children aged 10 to 14 had increased by 892 per cent since 2015, while infections among 15 to 19-year-olds had surged by 4183 per cent over the same period.

“Young people under the age of 25 accounted for 58 per cent of all new HIV cases recorded in 2025.”

Mr Naisele said injection drug use had emerged as the leading identified route of HIV transmission in Fiji.

“Injection drug use is the number one identified HIV transmission route,” he said, adding that needle sharing remained the most efficient way of transmitting the virus because “even microscopic blood residue is enough”.

He also warned that alcohol and drug use lowered inhibitions, making young people more likely to engage in unprotected sex or other risky behaviour.

Among school-age children, cannabis remained the most common drug of initiation, while methamphetamine use was growing, with injecting already reported among school-age users in Suva.

Mr Naisele said peer pressure, family dysfunction, trauma, poverty and limited adult supervision were among the factors making young people more vulnerable to both drug use and HIV infection.

He urged schools to strengthen counselling, referral systems and child protection measures to help identify vulnerable students before they fell deeper into addiction or HIV risk.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: GCC backs rule of law
item.description: THE Great Council of Chiefs (GCC) has called on iTaukei leaders and individuals to avoid making statements that threaten social cohesion and national stability. The council also rejected claims that the 50-year-old man under investigation for allegedly threatening to burn down Fiji’s Capital City in a social media post was a member of, or associated with, the GCC.
item.content:encoded:
THE Great Council of Chiefs (GCC) has called on iTaukei leaders and individuals to avoid making statements that threaten social cohesion and national stability.

The council also rejected claims that the 50-year-old man under investigation for allegedly threatening to burn down Fiji’s Capital City in a social media post was a member of, or associated with, the GCC.

In a statement, the council said it remained committed to promoting social cohesion, national stability and the prosperity of Fiji while continuing its role of advocating for the rightful authority of indigenous Fijians within the rule of law.

“In no way or manner has the GCC supported or encouraged such unruly statements by any individual or groups,” the council said.

Since its re-establishment in 2023, the GCC said only its chairman Ratu Viliame Seruvakula, or deputy chairpersons Ratu Suliano Matanitobua and Ratu Tevita Uluilakeba Mara in his absence, were authorised to speak on behalf of the council.

The GCC said any matter involving a breach of the law would be allowed to follow due process, as it would for any citizen.

It said members holding leadership positions must be positive influences and ensure their conduct remained within the law.

The council added that Fiji had moved beyond past political upheaval, hurt and trauma, and that all ethnic groups should now work towards unity and coexistence.

Meanwhile, police have suspended interviewing the man while awaiting the outcome of digital forensic analysis of his mobile phone.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fareed seeks bail pending sentence
item.description: A senior lawyer representing former Fijian Holdings Ltd (FHL) chief executive officer Mohammed Nouzab Fareed has applied for his client to be released on bail pending sentence. Mr Fareed, currently remanded in custody, did not appear in court this afternoon before Chief Magistrate Josaia Waqaivolavola.
item.content:encoded:
A senior lawyer representing former Fijian Holdings Ltd (FHL) chief executive officer Mohammed Nouzab Fareed has applied for his client to be released on bail pending sentence.

Mr Fareed, currently remanded in custody, did not appear in court this afternoon before Chief Magistrate Josaia Waqaivolavola.

His lawyers, Hemendra Nagin and Ritesh Singh, submitted that there was no evidence Mr Fareed would abscond, noting that he had complied with all previous bail conditions and attended every court hearing throughout the lengthy proceedings.

Principal legal officer Sadaf Shameem opposed the application, arguing that bail after conviction was not an automatic right but an exceptional measure.

Mr Waqaivolavola directed both the prosecution and defence to file simultaneous written submissions within three days, addressing whether the court has jurisdiction to grant bail pending sentence after conviction.

The Chief Magistrate will deliver his ruling on the bail application on July 30.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Province seeks divine help
item.description: SPIRITUAL intervention is needed to help address the growing social issues affecting communities, including drugs and HIV, says Roko Tui Dreketi Ro Teimumu Kepa. Speaking during the Rewa Provincial Council meeting yesterday, Ro Teimumu said delegates recommended fasting and prayer as part of the province’s response to the challenges confronting young people.
item.content:encoded:
SPIRITUAL intervention is needed to help address the growing social issues affecting communities, including drugs and HIV, says Roko Tui Dreketi Ro Teimumu Kepa.

Speaking during the Rewa Provincial Council meeting yesterday, Ro Teimumu said delegates recommended fasting and prayer as part of the province’s response to the challenges confronting young people.

“We need spiritual intervention for that.”

Ro Teimumu said the idea of fasting and prayer had been raised several years ago but did not receive much support at the time.

“Fasting and prayer were suggested years ago, but perhaps the time was not right because people looked at us as though we were talking about something unusual.”

She said attitudes had since changed as communities became increasingly concerned about the rise in drug abuse, HIV and AIDS, and other social issues.

“Everyone is now concerned about the challenges facing our young people, not only drugs but also HIV and AIDS and the many other social problems affecting our communities.”

During the meeting, delegates recommended that communities come together in prayer, including setting aside time between midnight and 3am each day.

Ro Teimumu said the challenges confronting the country could not be solved by one province alone and required both collective action and spiritual guidance.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Man urges government action over creek pollution
item.description: Illegal and indiscriminate rubbish dumping, and concerns over sewer discharge into a creek in Nabua along Ratu Mara Rd have become a growing environmental and public health issue, with a resident calling for urgent government intervention. Nabua resident Inoke Kelo, 64, said he had repeatedly complained to the Suva City Council about the polluted creek after he was informed that a sewer outlet pip
item.content:encoded:
Illegal and indiscriminate rubbish dumping, and concerns over sewer discharge into a creek in Nabua along Ratu Mara Rd have become a growing environmental and public health issue, with a resident calling for urgent government intervention.

Nabua resident Inoke Kelo, 64, said he had repeatedly complained to the Suva City Council about the polluted creek after he was informed that a sewer outlet pipe discharged into the waterway.

“I was told there is a 400mm sewer outlet pipe that empties into this creek,” he said.

During heavy rain, he said, rubbish from areas as far as Sotutu Place is washed downstream before piling up in the creek opposite his home.

“It piles up here, blocks the culvert and our area floods.”

The accumulated waste and polluted water often spill towards nearby homes, while residents and motorists travelling along Ratu Mara Rd are forced to endure an increasingly foul stench.

Mr Kelo, who has lived opposite the creek for the past decade, said the problem had persisted throughout this period.

“Now the only hope is for the Ministry of Environment to intervene and help the people living in this area.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Council revises Lautoka pool completion plans
item.description: THE Lautoka City Council is revising its scope of works for the yet to be completed Lautoka swimming pool. LCC special administrators’ chairperson Taitusi Rasoki said this was decided during a meeting yesterday to discuss the project’s future.
item.content:encoded:
THE Lautoka City Council is revising its scope of works for the yet to be completed Lautoka swimming pool.

LCC special administrators’ chairperson Taitusi Rasoki said this was decided during a meeting yesterday to discuss the project’s future.

“I can confirm we had been asked by the Local Government Ministry to look within the council’s finances for the completion of the project,’ he said.

“After our meeting today, we decided that the scope of works will be revised and we will discuss the new conditions with our project consultant.

“For now, I won’t be able to give a complete figure for the funding of the works that is yet to be done. That will depend on our scope of works.”

He said the focus would be shifted to completing critical components of the project.

“The critical components are the pool itself and other important facilities like electrical and plumbing works.

“Other planned noncritical components will come at a later stage.

He said the completed pool would not host international sporting events for now.

“We will look at the critical components first which is the pool and get that open for the public.

“Once we are open, then we will go into upgrading it to host international meets.”

Mr Rasoki said they hoped to complete the project next year.

“The bottom line is for the project to be completed early 2027.

“I had a discussion again with the minister and what we are going to do now is revise the scope of works and have our project consultant focus on the finishing the critical component of the project.”

The council was advised by the Local Government Ministry to look internally for further funding after the ministry’s submission for additional allocation under the 2026-2027 National Budget was snubbed earlier this year.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Sugar industry needs reform — Ratu Josefa
item.description: PEOPLE’S Alliance Party president Ratu Josefa Dimuri has called for major reforms in the sugar industry, including handing over the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) to a private operator and concentrating sugar production at the Labasa Mill. Ratu Josefa said the country needed to rethink the current structure of the industry, suggesting that sugar mills on Viti Levu should be closed and resources redi
item.content:encoded:
PEOPLE’S Alliance Party president Ratu Josefa Dimuri has called for major reforms in the sugar industry, including handing over the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) to a private operator and concentrating sugar production at the Labasa Mill.

Ratu Josefa said the country needed to rethink the current structure of the industry, suggesting that sugar mills on Viti Levu should be closed and resources redirected towards improving cane quality and increasing production in the North.

“I would like to suggest that we close the mills on Viti Levu and only concentrate sugar production at Labasa.

“We should improve the quality of the crop and increase production there,” he said.

His comments followed concerns raised by Sugar Minister Tomasi Tunabuna that the Government was investing heavily in the sugarcane sector while receiving limited returns.

Mr Tunabuna had also highlighted that the Government was paying about $18million annually towards debts owed to organisations, including the Exim Bank of India.

Ratu Josefa said the current situation required urgent change, comparing the industry to a cow that was failing to provide the expected returns.

“The cow the Government has at the moment has not given them the milk they expected.”

“I believe it is time to make that shift. We have to make that shift because we cannot afford to wait any longer.”

He said Fiji could not continue relying on the sugar industry without implementing major reforms to improve its sustainability and productivity.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PM Rabuka: Law must deal with alleged incident
item.description: PRIME Minister Sitiveni Rabuka says complaints about a viral video showing a preacher addressing students at a prominent secondary school in Lautoka should be referred to the relevant authorities, not directly to him, as investigations into the incident continue. Speaking to reporters, Mr Rabuka said he had read reports about the incident but had not watched the video.
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister Sitiveni Rabuka says complaints about a viral video showing a preacher addressing students at a prominent secondary school in Lautoka should be referred to the relevant authorities, not directly to him, as investigations into the incident continue.

Speaking to reporters, Mr Rabuka said he had read reports about the incident but had not watched the video.

He stressed that the Government operates through its agencies and that allegations arising from the incident should be handled by the appropriate enforcement bodies.

“Those that are aggrieved by the depiction in the video should take it to the right authorities, not the Prime Minister,” Mr Rabuka said.

He said concerns should be reported to agencies responsible for investigating possible breaches of the law.

“They take it to the police, they take it to the Online Safety Commission, they take it to all those, and it will eventually come to Government.”

He said while the Government was responsible for setting policies and passing legislation through Parliament, enforcement rested with the relevant departments.

“Government can only lay down the policies that are legislated in Parliament, but the actual execution of those things are done by those departments.”

Responding to suggestions that the matter raised constitutional issues, Mr Rabuka said the focus should remain on compliance with existing laws.

“What has the incident got to do with the Constitution?

“We just have the laws that people have to abide by. If anybody pushes it, then the law will have to push back, and that’s when they face the consequences.”

The video has attracted widespread public attention, with calls for authorities to investigate the circumstances under which the preacher was allowed to address students at the school.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
